narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 51.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to flag higher-risk applicants. Your profile shows strong personal traits like being a female Asian student from a low-income family background, planning to attend part-time, but it also highlights some academic hurdles that weighed heavily in the model's decision. The biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is much lower than the typical student's 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance might not prepare you well for the bar. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 25, well below the average of about 37, making it a major red flag for bar success. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also far under the usual 5.7, ranked third in influence and reinforced concerns about consistent early performance. Next came your racial background as Asian, which is less common in the dataset (only about 6% compared to 84% White students), subtly shifting the odds toward failure in this context. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, lower than the 3.2 average, followed closely, along with your low family income bracket versus the typical mid-range. Being part-time rather than full-time (only 7% of students) and female (44% of the group) had smaller but still negative pulls toward the failure prediction. Overall, these stacked up to tip the scales slightly against you compared to students who typically pass, who boast higher deciles, LSATs around 37, and GPAs over 3.2. The good news is several of these are changeable: retaking the LSAT to aim for 35 or higher could dramatically shift your profile, as could boosting your school deciles through stronger study habits in future terms. Focusing on full-time enrollment might also help align you with the majority who succeed. Consider targeted bar prep courses proven to lift low-LSAT folks, and building a higher undergrad-equivalent record if possible. With effort on these fronts, you could reapply stronger next cycle and flip this prediction. We're rooting for you—many have turned similar profiles around.
